briefing.incidentSummary:
A pharmaceutical company's entire R&D vault — 11 years of proprietary drug research — was encrypted and deleted overnight.

A ransom note demanded $2 million. But the encryption key was never sent, even after a partial payment was made.

This wasn't ransomware for money. This was destruction by design.

And it came from inside the network.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Trace the ransomware deployment

› Identify who planted the wiper disguised as ransomware

› Look for motive beyond the money

› Collect at least 4 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Ishaan M

digitalFootprint.locationTrace: PharmaCore R&D Building — Server Room Access

suspects.profileNotes: Conducted the last security audit of the vault system. Had full knowledge of the backup architecture. Was secretly employed by a rival pharma company — salary deposits traced to a shell account. The 'ransom' was theater; the goal was erasure.

suspects.name: Unknown Ransomware Group — IRON LOCK
suspects.profileNotes: IRON LOCK claimed responsibility. However their known ransomware signature includes a working decryption portal — none was provided here. The wiper code does not match any known IRON LOCK sample in threat intel databases.

suspects.name: Sneha P
suspects.profileNotes: Authorized partial ransom payment of $200K — a decision later scrutinized. No technical access to R&D vault. No nighttime activity.

suspects.name: Parth V
suspects.profileNotes: Manages vault infrastructure but access logs show no after-hours activity. Ishaan M used a vulnerability in the audit account — not Parth's credentials.

items.label: Your files are encrypted. Send $2M to wallet 3FZbgi29... within 48hrs.
items.detail: The ransom note was dropped in every encrypted directory. However threat intel shows IRON LOCK always provides a Tor-based decryption portal — no such portal exists here. The note is a decoy.

items.label: Delivery confirmed. The vault is dark. Expect your final payment.
items.detail: Recovered from an encrypted messaging app on Ishaan M's personal phone — seized during investigation. Sent at 4:22 AM — 7 minutes after the wiper completed. Confirms communication with an external party about the vault destruction.

items.label: Ishaan — I'm giving you full read/write on vault nodes for the security audit. Don't abuse it.
items.detail: Internal Slack message from 3 weeks before the attack. Parth gave Ishaan elevated vault access for the audit — access Ishaan never relinquished and later exploited.

items.label: wiper_analysis_report.pdf
items.detail: Malware analysis: the payload encrypted files with AES-256 but discarded the key locally and never transmitted it to a C2 server. True ransomware always retains the key for negotiation. This was a wiper. The ransomware wrapper was cosmetic.

items.label: payload_deployment_log.txt
items.detail: The wiper was deployed at 2:04 AM from IP 172.31.10.88 — Ishaan M's workstation — using his audit account credentials. Lateral movement across 44 vault nodes in 2 hours 11 minutes.

items.label: financial_investigation_report.pdf
items.detail: Ishaan M received 6 payments of ₹4.5 Lakh each over the past year from a shell company 'GreenPath Innovations' — traced to PharmaCore's primary competitor GenNova Ltd. A clear double employment arrangement.

hiddenItem.label: audit_backdoor_analysis.txt
hiddenItem.detail: During the security audit, Ishaan M installed a persistent backdoor on the vault controller — giving him persistent access outside of his audit credentials. The backdoor was specifically designed to survive a standard credential reset, confirming premeditation.

minigame.title: Decode Backdoor Author Signature
minigame.hint: Caesar shift -3. Who signed the backdoor?
hints: It is a researcher's name followed by their initial.

onSuccess.message: audit_backdoor_analysis.txt is now available in your file feed.

items.label: Backup Deletion Sequence
items.detail: All 3 backup tiers — local, NAS, and cloud — were wiped in sequence before the encryption ran. The attacker knew the exact backup architecture. Only the security auditor had mapped all three tiers.

items.label: C2 Communication Absent
items.detail: Network logs show zero outbound C2 communication during and after the attack — the encryption key was never sent anywhere. Real ransomware requires key exchange with the operator's server. This confirms the payload was always a wiper.

items.label: Wiper Origin — Internal Workstation
items.detail: IP 172.31.10.88 is the internal address of Ishaan M's assigned workstation in the R&D building. The wiper was deployed directly from this machine — no external entry point used.

timeline.title: Audit Access Granted
timeline.description: Ishaan M given full vault read/write for security audit

timeline.title: Backdoor Installed
timeline.description: Persistent backdoor placed on vault controller

timeline.title: Wiper Deployed
timeline.description: AES-256 wiper launched across 44 vault nodes

timeline.title: Vault Encrypted & Destroyed
timeline.description: 11 years of R&D data encrypted — key discarded

timeline.title: Completion Message Sent
timeline.description: Ishaan M sends 'Delivery confirmed' to unknown contact

timeline.title: Ransom Note Discovered
timeline.description: Staff find encrypted files and ransom demand

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Ishaan M exposed as a corporate spy who destroyed the vault.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: Correct suspect but the chain is incomplete.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Ishaan M.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...11 years of research. Gone in 2 hours.
The ransom note was a lie — there was never a key.
This was erasure. Find who was paid to erase it.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Malware analysis. Deployment logs. Financial traces.
The attacker was on the inside and on someone else's payroll.
aria.viewEvidence:
No C2 traffic. No key exchange. No decryption portal.
This was never ransomware. It was always a wiper.
aria.markEvidence: Good. The deception is unraveling layer by layer.
aria.decryptionHint:
The backdoor has a signature encoded inside it.
Decode the author.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
